natural_occurrence.description: Ethyl cellulose is a semi-synthetic derivative of natural cellulose. It's created by chemically modifying cellulose from wood pulp or cotton with ethyl groups, producing a material not found in nature.

manufacturing.description: Produced by treating alkali cellulose with ethyl chloride, replacing some hydroxyl groups with ethyl ether groups. Unlike methyl cellulose, ethyl cellulose is water-insoluble but soluble in organic solvents, making it useful for protective coatings and films.

other_uses.cosmetics: Film-former in nail polish, hair sprays, and cosmetics.
other_uses.industrial: Coatings, inks, adhesives, plastics modifier, hot-melt adhesives.
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Controlled-release tablet coating (water-insoluble barrier), microencapsulation, taste-masking.

eu.conditions: Approved for use in specified food categories; primarily used as film-forming agent.

commonly_found_in: Dietary supplement tablets and capsules (protective coating)
Less common in direct food use compared to other cellulose derivatives
fun_facts: Ethyl cellulose is basically waterproof modified wood fiber - it repels water rather than absorbing it like other celluloses
The coating on many time-release vitamin pills is ethyl cellulose - it slowly breaks down in your digestive system to release nutrients over time
Unlike methyl cellulose, ethyl cellulose doesn't dissolve in water at all, making it perfect for moisture-resistant coatings
You're eating wood pulp that's been chemically treated to make it water-repellent - nature's plastic coating!
Because it's indigestible and water-insoluble, it passes through your digestive system completely unchanged
